---
title: "Lesson 1: HuggingFace Beyond Upload"
subtitle: "Run Qwen3 0.6B locally, tour HuggingFace properly, and see open models as a supply chain."
description: "The full Lesson 1: the three intuitions about LLMs, why Qwen3 0.6B is the right teaching model, a guided tour of HuggingFace (LFS → Xet, model cards, config.json, Transformers), markdown for AI workflows, the math question, running Qwen3 in pure C with GGUF + system prompts + thinking + multi-turn, the training trajectory, and the new lens — HuggingFace as a supply chain (provenance, datasets, security, evals, runtime, agents). The companion video mirrors this page."
page-layout: full
css:
  - ../styles/journey.css
  - ../styles/lessons.css
---

This is the **full lesson** — everything you need is on this page. The video mirrors what's written here; the transcript is interactive, so you can click any line to jump the video to that point. Read it, watch it, do the homework, bring it to office hours.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Video placeholder.** The Lesson 1 recording is being produced. The player below is wired with the Lesson 0 video for now so chapters and interactive transcript work end-to-end while you read along — the new recording drops into the same slot when it's published.
:::

## Navigate by roadmap

| Step | Topic | This lesson |
|------|-------|-------------|
| [Lesson 0](lesson-0-welcome.qmd) | Welcome to First Break AI | — |
| **Lesson 1** | **HuggingFace Beyond Upload** | **You are here** |
| [Step 2](../roadmap.qmd) | Run a model locally | Builds directly on this lesson |

[← Back to Lessons](index.qmd) · [← Back to Roadmap](../roadmap.qmd)


```{=html}
<div class="lesson-page lesson-page-theatre" data-size="compact">

  <div class="lesson-deck">
    <div class="lesson-video-wrap">
      <div id="ytPlayer"
           data-video-id="r9uykyGAdJQ"
           data-transcript="lesson-1-huggingface-beyond-upload.json"></div>
    </div>

    <div class="lesson-meta">
      <div class="lesson-now-playing">
        <span class="lesson-now-label">Chapter</span>
        <span class="lesson-chapter-title" id="lessonChapterTitle">The shallow view, and the deep view</span>
      </div>
      <div class="lesson-controls">
        <div class="lesson-size-group" role="group" aria-label="Video size">
          <button type="button" class="lesson-size-btn" data-size="compact">Compact</button>
          <button type="button" class="lesson-size-btn" data-size="theatre">Theatre</button>
          <button type="button" class="lesson-size-btn" data-size="wide">Wide</button>
        </div>
        <button type="button" class="transcript-toggle" id="lessonTranscriptToggle">Show Transcript</button>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="lesson-chapters">
      <div class="lesson-chapters-label">Chapters</div>
      <div class="lesson-chapter-list" id="lessonChapterList"></div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="scene-transcript lesson-transcript" id="lessonTranscript"></div>

</div>
```

---

## The shape of this lesson

This lesson has two halves stacked on top of each other.

**Half one — Run a model locally (the practical core).** Everything you need to actually run Qwen3 0.6B on your laptop and have a working mental model of what's happening: the three intuitions, why we picked Qwen3 0.6B, the HuggingFace ecosystem tour, `config.json` + the Transformers library, the live pure-C demo, and the training trajectory. This is the May 8 office hours, made detailed and reproducible.

**Half two — HuggingFace as a supply chain (the new lens).** Once you can *run* a model, the next professional skill is *inspecting* one. A model release is not a checkpoint file — it's a software supply chain with provenance, datasets, security, evals, runtime, and agent-readable layers. This is what separates someone who uses HuggingFace from someone who understands it.

The homework at the end forces you to apply both halves to a single model.

---

# Half one — Run a model locally

## 1. Three intuitions about LLMs

If you only walk away with three things, walk away with these. Every concept in the cohort — tokenization, attention, training loss, sampling — sits on top of one of them.

### Intuition 1 — A model is a stack of repeated blocks

Open any modern LLM and the architecture has the same skeleton: an input → an embedding lookup → a tower of identical decoder blocks → a normalisation step → a linear projection → an output. Qwen3-0.6B looks like this:

![Qwen3-0.6B interactive model view — 24 nodes, 9 op types, 151,936-token vocab. The decoder block repeats x28.](../public/images/office-hours/2026-05-08-qwen3-model-view.png){fig-alt="Qwen3 0.6B architecture diagram: input → text embeddings → Qwen3DecoderLayer x28 → RMSNorm → Linear → output"}

What to notice:

- **`Qwen3DecoderLayer × 28`** — the entire "depth" of the model is the same block stacked 28 times. Once you understand one block (attention + MLP + normalisation), you understand the whole tower.
- **`151,936` tokens** — that is the vocabulary size. Every input word/symbol/sub-word maps to one of these IDs.
- **Operation breakdown** — `Linear` is ~36% of operations and `RMSNorm` ~23%. Most of an LLM is matrix multiplications and normalisations; the "magic" lives in the weights, not in exotic operators.

Explore this interactively at **[hfviewer.com/Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B](https://hfviewer.com/Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B)**.

### Intuition 2 — LLMs are autoregressive: one token at a time

LLMs do not "write a sentence." They generate **one token, append it to the input, then generate the next token**. That loop is called *autoregressive decoding*. Whether you ask a question, ask for code, or ask for a full app, the shape is always the same: input → some processing → output, one token at a time.

```text
prompt: "Write a sonnet about love."
step 1  →  "In"
step 2  →  "In twilight's"
step 3  →  "In twilight's hush,"
step 4  →  "In twilight's hush, where"
... and so on
```

In pseudocode the whole generation loop is roughly:

```python
tokens = tokenizer.encode(prompt)        # [list of int]
while not done(tokens):
    logits = model.forward(tokens)       # shape: [vocab_size]
    next_id = sample(logits)             # one int from vocab
    tokens.append(next_id)
    yield tokenizer.decode([next_id])    # stream this back
```

Every architectural detail you'll learn in this cohort — the KV cache, attention masks, sampling temperature, speculative decoding — exists to make *that loop* run efficiently and controllably.

The InfoLens space makes the loop visible — every generated token connects back to the prompt tokens it was paying attention to:

![InfoLens — context-attribution DAG. Each generated token (e.g. "gentle") traces back to the prompt tokens that informed it.](../public/images/office-hours/2026-05-08-infolens-attribution.png){fig-alt="InfoLens HuggingFace space showing context-attribution DAG and info highlight panel"}

Try it live: **[huggingface.co/spaces/dqy08/InfoLens](https://huggingface.co/spaces/dqy08/InfoLens)**.

### Intuition 3 — The model outputs a probability distribution over the *entire* vocabulary

This is the intuition learners miss most often.

When the model is "predicting the next token," it is *not* picking one word. At every step it produces a probability for **every single one of the ~150K vocabulary tokens** (151,936 for Qwen3). The "output" you see is just the result of *sampling* from that distribution.

```text
At step k, the model emits a vector of length 151,936:

  token_id 0       →  0.0001
  token_id 1       →  0.0003
  ...
  token_id 9821    →  0.342   ("twilight")   ← high
  token_id 9822    →  0.118   ("twinkle")
  ...
  token_id 151935  →  0.0000

Sampling step picks one. Greedy = pick the max.
Temperature / top-p / top-k = different ways of "shaping"
this distribution before sampling.
```

This is why `temperature` and `top_p` can change a model's output without changing the model: they reshape how aggressively we sample from this distribution.

> **Mental-model lock.** A model is a stack of identical blocks (Intuition 1) that repeatedly turns a context into a vocab-sized probability vector (Intuition 3) and we sample from that vector one token at a time (Intuition 2). Everything else in the cohort is detail filling in those three pictures.

---

## 2. Why Qwen3 0.6B is the right model to learn from

A common reaction: *"600 million parameters? Isn't that tiny? GPT-4 is hundreds of billions."*

Yes — and that is the point.

| Model class | Parameters | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Frontier (Claude, GPT-4-class) | **200B – 300B+** | Closed weights, served via API |
| Mid-scale open | 7B – 70B | Llama, Mistral, Qwen mid-tier |
| **Qwen3-0.6B** | **600M** | What we run in this cohort |

The architecture of Qwen3-0.6B is **structurally the same** as Claude-class models — same decoder-only transformer, same attention, same positional encoding family, same sampling. The differences are:

- **Scale** — number of layers, hidden dim, attention heads.
- **Training data** — Qwen3-0.6B was trained on far less data than a frontier model.

Because the *shape* is identical, learning Qwen3-0.6B teaches you the same mental model you need for the big ones — but with weights small enough to fit on a laptop, run on a CPU, and read end-to-end. **It is the stepping stone for everything else in the roadmap.**

> *Why does this matter for teaching?* A 7B model is too big to walk through line-by-line in a session. A 600M model is small enough that we will, in this cohort, walk every layer, every tensor shape, and every line of inference code. The lessons transfer up; the size does not get in the way.

---

## 3. HuggingFace — the GitHub for models

If you understand GitHub, you already understand most of HuggingFace.

| GitHub | HuggingFace |
|---|---|
| Source code repos | **Models** repos |
| `README.md` | **Model card** (still a `README.md`) |
| Issues, PRs, branches | Issues, PRs, branches |
| Stars, forks | Likes, downloads |
| Releases | **Model files** (`.safetensors`, `.bin`, `.gguf`) |
| Datasets (sometimes) | **Datasets** as a first-class section |
| GitHub Pages | **Spaces** (interactive demos) |

Three top-level sections to know on the HF site:

- **Models** — the LLMs themselves.
- **Datasets** — the training/eval data.
- **Spaces** — runnable demos (we used [InfoLens](https://huggingface.co/spaces/dqy08/InfoLens) earlier).

### Scale check

When you publish an open model, you are publishing infrastructure. One older model alone hit **~18 million downloads in a single month**. Good model providers (the Qwen team is one) write detailed model cards: prompt template, supported languages, eval numbers, known limitations. Bad providers ship just weights and let you guess. The quality of the model card is one of the best signals of how seriously a release is being maintained.

### Why HuggingFace exists at all — the large-blob problem

Source code is small text files. **Models are gigabytes of binary weights.** GitHub has a **~100 MB hard limit on individual files** without special tooling — push a 5 GB `.safetensors` to vanilla Git and the push fails.

GitHub solved part of this with **Git LFS** (Large File Storage) — a layer on top of Git that stores big blobs separately and replaces them in the repo with small pointer files. HuggingFace started here too. They have since moved beyond LFS to **Xet**, a content-addressed storage system designed specifically for the access patterns of model files: chunk-level dedup, faster downloads, better behaviour for the *every-five-minutes-someone-pulls-7-billion-parameters* workload. When you see a model file marked with the Xet indicator on a HF repo, that is what is happening underneath.

### Anatomy of a model repo

Open [`Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B`](https://huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B) and you will see something like:

```text
Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B/
├── README.md                    # the model card — license, prompt format, evals, examples
├── config.json                  # the architecture spec (Topic 4 below)
├── tokenizer.json               # the tokenizer's vocabulary and rules
├── tokenizer_config.json        # tokenizer settings (chat template, special tokens)
├── generation_config.json       # default sampling params (temperature, top-p)
├── special_tokens_map.json      # how special tokens map to vocab IDs
├── model.safetensors            # THE WEIGHTS — billions of floats
└── (sometimes) *.gguf           # CPU-friendly alternative format
```

Three of those files do almost all the work:

- `README.md` is the **model card** — a markdown document. The model card is the closest thing to a contract between the model author and you.
- `config.json` is the **architecture spec** — width, depth, heads, vocab size. Without it the weights file is a meaningless block of bytes.
- `model.safetensors` is the **weights** — billions of floats, stored in a format that is safe to load (no arbitrary code execution) and fast to memory-map.

We'll spend the next section on `config.json` because it is the one most learners skim past.

---

## 4. `config.json` + the Transformers library

`config.json` is the single most important small file in a model repo. **It describes the architecture** — number of layers, hidden size, number of attention heads, vocabulary size, RoPE settings, activation function — everything you need to *reconstruct the model in code* before you load the weights.

```text
weights file (.safetensors)  →  just numbers
config.json                  →  the shape they go into
```

Without `config.json`, the bytes in `.safetensors` are meaningless. Here is a (truncated) example of what Qwen3-0.6B's looks like:

```json
{
  "architectures": ["Qwen3ForCausalLM"],
  "hidden_size": 1024,
  "intermediate_size": 2816,
  "num_attention_heads": 16,
  "num_hidden_layers": 28,
  "num_key_value_heads": 8,
  "vocab_size": 151936,
  "max_position_embeddings": 32768,
  "rope_theta": 1000000.0,
  "torch_dtype": "bfloat16"
}
```

Match the numbers up against the architecture diagram from Intuition 1 — `num_hidden_layers: 28` is your `Qwen3DecoderLayer × 28`. `hidden_size: 1024` is the dimensional width of every tensor flowing through the tower. `vocab_size: 151936` is the size of the probability vector at every step. **The config tells you the shape of the thing before you ever touch the weights.**

### The Transformers library

[**`transformers`**](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers) — the library, created and maintained by HuggingFace — is the standard way to use these models from Python. The pattern is always the same:

```python
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B")
model     = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B")

inputs  = tokenizer("Why does attention work?", return_tensors="pt")
outputs = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=200)
print(tokenizer.decode(outputs[0]))
```

What `transformers` does behind that two-line load:

1. Reads `config.json` from the repo.
2. Looks at `"architectures": ["Qwen3ForCausalLM"]` and picks the right Python class — [the source is here](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/main/src/transformers/models/qwen3/modeling_qwen3.py#L442).
3. Builds the architecture *in PyTorch* from the config.
4. Streams the weights from `model.safetensors` into the right tensor slots.
5. Returns a ready-to-call PyTorch model.

The rest of the cohort sits on top of that loop. Step 3 (inference) is *making it faster*. Step 4 (training) is *training one of your own*. Step 5 (product) is *building around it*.

> **Practical demo for learners.** Open the [HuggingFace Qwen3 docs](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/v5.8.0/en/model_doc/qwen3#transformers.Qwen3ForCausalLM) on one screen and the [`modeling_qwen3.py` source](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/main/src/transformers/models/qwen3/modeling_qwen3.py#L442) on the other. The docs are what they see; the source is what is actually running. Switch between them while you teach.

---

## 5. Markdown everywhere — and Karpathy's AI knowledge base

Model cards are markdown. Issues and PRs on GitHub are markdown. Discord posts use markdown. Even the task lists you write for yourself in your IDE are markdown.

Markdown is not a "documentation format" — it is the **lingua franca of AI-assisted work**. If you are not fluent in it, you will spend the cohort fighting your own tooling.

The basics, with a quick sandbox to try them in: **[dillinger.io](https://dillinger.io/)** — a browser-based markdown editor with live preview. Write headings, lists, links, code blocks, tables. Five minutes is enough.

### Karpathy's evolving knowledge base

A learner asked about Andrej Karpathy's recent suggestion to "use markdown files as a database."

The clarification: Karpathy is **not** advocating markdown as a relational database. He is describing **a personal, evolving knowledge base** — a tree of markdown files that an AI agent (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) can iterate on, enrich, and re-organise as your understanding grows. The AI reads, writes, and restructures markdown the way a junior researcher would update a notebook. The format is markdown precisely because:

- It is human-readable when the AI is wrong.
- It diffs cleanly in Git.
- Every model on Earth has been trained to read and write it fluently.

This is a workflow recommendation, not a data-storage architecture. The takeaway for the cohort: **start a markdown notebook now**. Drop everything you learn into it. Let your AI tools enrich and reorganise it as you go.

---

## 6. Do I need deep math to contribute to open source?

A learner raised this honestly: *"I do not have a strong math background. Should I even be in this cohort? Can I contribute to open source without that?"*

**Short answer: no, you do not need deep math up front. We go in reverse.**

1. **Start from inference** — get Qwen3 running locally, watch it generate tokens. This needs *zero* math beyond reading numbers off a screen.
2. **Add concepts only when they unlock something practical.** When attention starts mattering for what we are doing, we cover attention. When RoPE matters, we cover RoPE. Not before.
3. **Math becomes legible because you have already seen the thing it describes.** Reading the attention equations after you have watched 28 decoder layers run on your laptop is a very different experience from reading them cold.

This is the opposite of how a typical ML course is structured (math → code). It is the same approach Karpathy uses in his "Let's reproduce GPT" lectures, and it is how most working ML engineers actually learnt — by running models, breaking them, and *then* picking up the math piece by piece.

If you have the math, great — it will accelerate you. If you do not, that is fine — you can ship real work in this field without it. **What you cannot skip is willingness to read code and run experiments.**

---

## 7. Running Qwen3 0.6B locally — the pure-C demo

Section 4 covered loading via `transformers` (Python + PyTorch). For learning, we go further: **ignore `transformers` entirely** and run the model with a tiny C program. This is the cohort's "from first principles" approach.

### Why pure C?

- **No abstractions to hide behind** — every step from token IDs to logits is in code you can read.
- **No GPU required** — the demo runs on your laptop's CPU.
- **Same architecture, just spelt out** — the C code is functionally an exact version of what `Qwen3ForCausalLM` does inside `transformers`.

### GGUF — the file format we use here

Instead of `.safetensors` (the format `transformers` likes), we use **`.gguf`** — a single self-contained file that bundles weights, tokenizer, and config in a CPU-friendly layout. GGUF is the format of choice for tools like `llama.cpp` and is what makes "run a real LLM on a laptop CPU" tractable.

The differences in one table:

| | `.safetensors` | `.gguf` |
|---|---|---|
| Bundles weights + tokenizer + config? | No (separate files) | **Yes (single file)** |
| Designed for | GPU inference via PyTorch | **CPU inference** |
| Loaded by | `transformers`, `vllm` | `llama.cpp`, `ollama`, our pure-C binary |
| Quantization built in | Limited | **First-class** |
| Memory-mapped | Yes | Yes |

### The cohort repo

**[github.com/thefirehacker/QWEN3-RunLocally](https://github.com/thefirehacker/QWEN3-RunLocally)** — clone it, follow the README.

```bash
git clone https://github.com/thefirehacker/QWEN3-RunLocally
cd QWEN3-RunLocally
make
./run \
  --model qwen3-0.6b.gguf \
  --system "You are an AI expert running orientation for new hires at Accenture." \
  --thinking on \
  --multi-turn on
```

What that one command stitches together:

1. Loads the `.gguf` (weights + tokenizer + config in one file).
2. Applies the `--system` prompt as the model's *role* before any user input.
3. Generates one token at a time on the CPU (Intuition 2).
4. Keeps conversation history between turns (`--multi-turn on`).
5. Optionally produces an internal reasoning trace (`--thinking on`).

### System prompts — the Accenture demo

The `--system` flag lets you set the role the model adopts before any user message. In the live office hours we made the model an *"AI expert running orientation for new hires at Accenture,"* and a 600M-parameter model produced perfectly competent answers about transformer architecture, attention, and training. **The architecture is the same as Claude's; only the scale and training data differ.** Once you have seen this with your own eyes, large models stop feeling magical.

This is the moment in the lesson you should *demo live* if you are teaching it. Type a system prompt that matches your audience (their company, their team, their job role), ask the model an in-domain question, and watch a tiny model give a competent answer. It changes how learners think about what's possible on commodity hardware.

### `--thinking` and `--multi-turn` — your first hyperparameter levers

| Flag | What it does |
|---|---|
| `--thinking on` | Enables the model's *internal reasoning trace* (the `<think>...</think>` block). The model spends extra compute "thinking out loud" before producing its real answer. Same idea as the deliberation step you see in ChatGPT-style products. **Costs more compute → typically gives better answers.** |
| `--multi-turn on` | Keeps conversation history between turns. With it off, every prompt is a fresh start. With it on, the model carries the previous turns as context — and you start to see the KV cache earn its keep. |

The takeaway: **hyperparameters are levers**. `thinking` trades compute for quality. Sampling temperature trades determinism for creativity. Most of the "tuning" you will do for the rest of the cohort is some flavour of this same trade.

---

## 8. From random weights to Qwen3 0.6B — the training trajectory

Today, `Qwen3-0.6B` has 600M parameters and writes coherent essays. **It started with random numbers.** Every weight in that 600M-parameter tensor was initialised from a random distribution.

So how does a random model become Qwen3?

```text
Random weights
  ↓  pretrain on trillions of tokens (next-token prediction)
Base model — fluent text continuation, no instruction following
  ↓  supervised fine-tuning (instruction–response pairs)
Instruct model — answers questions, follows instructions
  ↓  preference optimisation (DPO / RLHF / similar)
Aligned model — preferred outputs, refuses harmful requests
  ↓  reasoning / "thinking" training (long chain-of-thought traces)
Thinking model — Qwen3 with <think>...</think> on
```

What changes at each stage:

| Stage | Data shape | What the model learns |
|---|---|---|
| **Pretraining** | Raw text (web, code, books) | Statistical structure of language — the *next token* task |
| **SFT** | `(prompt, ideal response)` pairs | To produce *answers*, not just continuations |
| **Preference** | `(prompt, better, worse)` triples | Which of two valid responses humans prefer |
| **Reasoning** | Long traces with `<think>` segments | When and how to "think out loud" before answering |

**Key takeaway for Cohort 01.** When we train our own small GPT in Step 4, we are reproducing **only the first stage** — pretraining — at toy scale. That is the hardest stage to get right and the one that defines almost all of a model's capability. Everything later is shaping a model that already exists; pretraining *creates* it.

---

# Half two — The new lens: HuggingFace as a supply chain

You can now run a model. The next professional skill is *inspecting* one.

## 9. A model is not a file. It is a node in a graph.

The shallow mental model:

```text
Find model on HuggingFace
  ↓  download
  ↓  run
```

The professional mental model:

```text
Paper
  ↓
Base model
  ↓
Pretraining data
  ↓
SFT data
  ↓
Preference data
  ↓
Quantizations (GGUF variants, GPTQ, AWQ)
  ↓
Fine-tunes (community + official)
  ↓
Spaces and demos that use it
  ↓
Apps deployed on top of it
  ↓
Benchmarks
  ↓
Discussions and issues
```

When you see `Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B` on a model page, that one line is the **tip of a tree**. Underneath are paper releases, dataset cards, evaluation runs, derivative quantizations, hosted spaces, third-party fine-tunes, and discussions.

A model hub *looks* like a website. Underneath, it's a **graph database** — models, datasets, spaces, papers, collections, eval results, commits, licences.

A good model page should answer four questions: *What is this. Where did it come from. What depends on it. What can I safely do with it.* If a page doesn't answer those — that's a signal about the model itself.

## 10. The engine and the car

A model file is like an **engine**.

But a car is not an engine. A car needs a chassis. A fuel system. A safety certificate. A user manual. A service history. A dashboard. A road test. Ownership papers. A repair network.

An engine on the floor of a garage is not a car. It is a component.

An open model is the same. The weights are the engine. To turn them into something trustworthy and usable, you need a config, a tokenizer, metadata, a licence, evals, dataset lineage, a safety scan, runtime options, a demo, and community feedback.

Each of those is a **layer of the supply chain**. Each layer is something you can inspect.

## 11. The supply-chain layers (a quick tour)

| Layer | What it answers |
|---|---|
| **Artifact** | What files actually define the model? |
| **Storage** | How are multi-GB blobs stored and downloaded? (LFS → Xet) |
| **Metadata** | Can humans *and* machines understand the model? |
| **Provenance** | Where did this model come from? Official or derivative? |
| **Dataset** | What data shaped it? What benchmarks evaluated it? |
| **Security** | Is it safe to load and run? Does it need custom code? |
| **Eval** | Are the model's claims reproducible? Where are the raw outputs? |
| **Runtime** | How will the model actually be executed? (`transformers`, `llama.cpp`, vLLM, hosted) |
| **Compute** | Where does inference / evaluation run? (CPU, GPU, serverless) |
| **Distribution** | How does the model get discovered? |
| **Agent** | Can an AI agent inspect and use this model without a human? |
| **Governance** | Who can access it and under what licence? |

You don't need to master every layer in this lesson. You need to know they exist, and you need three of them deeply now: **security**, **evals**, and **agent-readable manifests**.

## 12. Layer deep dive — Security and `trust_remote_code`

The most important sentence of this lesson:

> When you write `trust_remote_code=True` in `from_pretrained`, you are giving a stranger permission to run their Python code on your machine.

Not metaphorically. Literally. The flag downloads `modeling_*.py` files from the repo and executes them on import.

Beginners hit a load error, paste `trust_remote_code=True`, the load works, and they move on. That's not a workaround. **That's a security decision.**

### File tiering — internalise this

| Tier | Files | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Low risk** | `config.json`, `tokenizer.json`, `tokenizer_config.json`, `special_tokens_map.json`, `generation_config.json`, `model.safetensors`, `README.md` | These are *data*, not *code*. Safetensors deliberately cannot execute code on load. |
| **Higher risk** | `pytorch_model.bin` (pickle), `modeling_*.py`, `tokenization_*.py`, `requirements.txt`, `app.py` | Pickle can execute arbitrary code on load. Custom Python runs when you pass `trust_remote_code=True`. `requirements.txt` may pull untrusted packages. |

The professional habit: **before you load a model from an unfamiliar repo, read its file list.** If you see custom Python or pickle weights, slow down. Read the code. Or pick a different repo.

Most popular models — Qwen, Llama, Mistral, the well-known ones — ship safetensors and need no custom code. *That is a feature.* That is what you want.

```python
# Reasonable default — no custom code allowed
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B",
    # trust_remote_code defaults to False — keep it that way
)

# If a model REQUIRES trust_remote_code, treat it like running a stranger's binary.
# Read the .py files in the repo first. Or look for a safetensors-only alternative.
```

## 13. Layer deep dive — Evals and reproducibility

A leaderboard without reproducibility is just marketing.

Open any model card. You will see phrases like *strong reasoning, better coding, improved instruction following, fast inference, small but powerful*. Train yourself to ask:

```text
Compared to what?
On which benchmark?
With which prompt format?
With which decoding settings?
On which hardware?
Can I reproduce it?
Where are the raw outputs?
```

A serious evaluation isn't a single number. It's a pipeline:

```text
Benchmark dataset
  ↓
Prompt template
  ↓
Model runner (which runtime, what parameters)
  ↓
Raw generations saved
  ↓
Scoring script
  ↓
Metrics
  ↓
Result file
  ↓
Leaderboard
  ↓
Reproducibility notes (hardware, seeds, versions)
```

If a model claims a score and you can't find the prompts used, the decoding parameters, or the raw outputs — treat the claim as advertising, not data.

And **measure more than accuracy**. In real product work, latency, tokens/sec, memory used, cost per 100 prompts, format-following score, and refusal behaviour all matter as much as the headline benchmark number. The cheapest, fastest model that hits 90% of the quality often beats the prettiest leaderboard number that costs ten times more to serve.

## 14. Layer deep dive — Agent-readable manifests

This is the layer most people haven't heard of yet, and the one that's going to matter most over the next two years.

Every model page on HuggingFace today is written for a *human* reading a screen. `README.md` files, prose paragraphs, screenshots. That's fine for humans.

But more and more model selection — for code completion, agent tools, RAG pipelines — is being done by **AI agents calling APIs, not humans clicking links**.

A `README.md` is for humans:

```md
This model is useful for educational text-generation demos. It's small enough to run on a laptop and works well for...
```

An agent needs structured fields:

```json
{
  "repo": "firstbreak/qwen-mini",
  "task": "text-generation",
  "parameter_count": 600000000,
  "weight_formats": ["safetensors", "gguf"],
  "recommended_runtimes": ["transformers", "llama.cpp", "vllm"],
  "safe_to_load": true,
  "requires_trust_remote_code": false,
  "license": "apache-2.0",
  "intended_use": ["education", "local inference demo"],
  "not_recommended_for": ["medical advice", "legal advice", "high-risk decisions"],
  "evals": [
    { "benchmark": "firstbreak-ai-systems-mini", "score": 0.72, "date": "2026-05-12" }
  ]
}
```

When you build something in this cohort, ask: *could an AI agent figure out how to use this without a human in the loop?* If the answer is no, you've left work on the table.

This is also the bridge to where the cohort is heading in Step 5 (build an AI product). The products that win the next two years will be the ones that are *legible to other agents*, not just to humans.

---

## Why this matters — the one-line takeaway

> A model hub is not a folder of files. It is a **trust, compute, metadata, and distribution layer** for AI artifacts. Most people use it. You're going to understand it.

The cohort's whole second half — Step 3 (inference), Step 4 (training), Step 5 (product), Step 6 (capstone) — sits on top of this mental model. Get it right now and everything that follows clicks.

---

## Companion reading

- **[Office Hours — 8 May 2026](../office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd)** — the live session this lesson is built from. Topics 1–4 are the prerequisites for half one; the supply-chain framing in half two is new.
- **[Run Qwen3 0.6B in pure C](../blog/qwen3-run-locally.qmd)** — what's inside the `.gguf` file we ran.
- **[GGUF vs SafeTensors](../blog/model-formats-gguf-safetensors.qmd)** — the weight-format half of the supply chain.
- **[Karpathy — Let's reproduce GPT-2 (124M)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=l8pRSuU81PU)** — the lecture this cohort is modelled on.
- **[`modeling_qwen3.py` (transformers)](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/main/src/transformers/models/qwen3/modeling_qwen3.py#L442)** — the implementation we dissect in later lessons.

---

## Homework — the Supply-Chain Audit

Pick one model on HuggingFace. **Recommended:** [`Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B`](https://huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B), since we're using it through the rest of the cohort.

Write a short blog post on your Quarto blog answering these eleven questions for that model:

1. **Where did it come from?** Paper, base model, organisation. (Look for a verified badge and a paper link.)
2. **What files define it?** List the repo's top-level files. Identify which are *data* vs *code*.
3. **Can I inspect the dataset it was trained on?** Is the pretraining corpus listed? Is the SFT data available?
4. **Can I reproduce its benchmark claims?** Where are the raw outputs? Which prompts, which decoding parameters?
5. **Is it safe to load?** Are the weights `.safetensors` or pickle?
6. **Does it require `trust_remote_code=True`?** If yes — what does that code do? (Read it.)
7. **What licence applies?** Can I use it commercially? Is attribution required?
8. **Can I run it locally?** What runtime fits — `transformers`, `llama.cpp`, vLLM? Is there a `.gguf` variant?
9. **Can I deploy it?** What's the cheapest path to a working endpoint?
10. **What linked artifacts exist?** Spaces, derivative quantizations, datasets, papers, collections.
11. **Could an AI agent answer all of these by reading the repo?** What's missing? What would a `model_context.json` for this model look like?

Drop the post in `#blog-share` on Discord when it's up. Bring the audit to Friday's office hours and we'll dissect it live.

---

## Next steps

1. **Watch** the lesson video with the transcript open. Click around chapters until the mental model sticks.
2. **Do** the Supply-Chain Audit homework above — pick a model, answer all eleven questions, publish.
3. **Run** Qwen3 0.6B locally using [QWEN3-RunLocally](https://github.com/thefirehacker/QWEN3-RunLocally). Try your own system prompt with `--thinking on` and `--multi-turn on`.
4. **Continue** to **[Step 2 — Run a model locally](../roadmap.qmd)** with the deeper frame in mind: every model you load is a supply-chain artifact.
